title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: Install bayescomp make sure cmdstanr configured (bayescomp uses Stan backend):
code: library(bayescomp) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: example data ships package. compares two NEB algorithms (CI-NEB OCI-NEB) 24 transition-state search problems. row one system; columns like Calls_CINEB Calls_MMF hold force evaluation counts. Read . columns use suffixes _CINEB _MMF (internal label OCI-NEB). bc_read_benchmark() reads CSV, converts system column factor, attaches metadata attributes downstream functions use automatically.
code: path <- system.file("extdata", "baker_bench.csv", package = "bayescomp")  bench_raw <- bc_read_benchmark(   path,   format         = "wide",   method_suffixes = c("CINEB", "MMF"),   system_col     = "System",   count_col      = "Calls",   time_col       = "Time",   success_col    = "Term" )  dim(bench_raw) #> [1] 24 27 names(bench_raw) #>  [1] "System"                "Reaction"              "Formula"               #>  [4] "N_Atoms"               "Total_Mass"            "E_Diff"                #>  [7] "Barrier_Diff"          "RMSD_Saddle"           "Ratio_Calls"           #> [10] "Ratio_Time"            "RMSD_Init_Final_CINEB" "RMSD_Init_Final_MMF"   #> [13] "Barrier_CINEB"         "Force_CINEB"           "Path_Len_CINEB"        #> [16] "Calls_CINEB"           "Time_CINEB"            "Term_CINEB"            #> [19] "Force_Init_CINEB"      "Force_Final_CINEB"     "Barrier_MMF"           #> [22] "Force_MMF"             "Calls_MMF"             "Time_MMF"              #> [25] "Term_MMF"              "Force_Init_MMF"        "Force_Final_MMF"

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: statistical models expect one observation per row. bc_pivot_long() reshapes wide data system-method pair becomes row. method_levels argument controls factor ordering. first level ("CINEB") becomes reference method model, treatment effects reported relative CI-NEB. can inspect structure make sure looks right: Expected output (abbreviated):
code: bench_long <- bc_pivot_long(   bench_raw,   method_pattern = "_(CINEB|MMF)$",   method_levels  = c("CINEB", "MMF"),   count_col      = "count",   time_col       = "time",   success_col    = "success",   success_value  = "GOOD" )  head(bench_long) #> # A tibble: 6 × 21 #>   system_id Reaction            Formula N_Atoms Total_Mass   E_Diff Barrier_Diff #>   <fct>     <chr>               <chr>     <int>      <dbl>    <dbl>        <dbl> #> 1 01_hcn    bold('01:')~HCN*'-… CHN           3       27.0  3.00e-6  -0.00000900 #> 2 01_hcn    bold('01:')~HCN*'-… CHN           3       27.0  3.00e-6  -0.00000900 #> 3 02_hcch   bold('02:')~HCCH*'… C2H2          4       26.0 -5.84e-4  -0.000595   #> 4 02_hcch   bold('02:')~HCCH*'… C2H2          4       26.0 -5.84e-4  -0.000595   #> 5 03_h2co   bold('03:')~H[2]*C… CH2O          4       30.0 -2.70e-3  -0.00271    #> 6 03_h2co   bold('03:')~H[2]*C… CH2O          4       30.0 -2.70e-3  -0.00271    #> # ℹ 14 more variables: RMSD_Saddle <dbl>, Ratio_Calls <dbl>, Ratio_Time <dbl>, #> #   method <fct>, RMSD_Init_Final <dbl>, Barrier <dbl>, Force <dbl>, #> #   Path_Len <dbl>, count <int>, time <dbl>, term_reason <chr>, #> #   Force_Init <dbl>, Force_Final <dbl>, success <lgl> str(bench_long[, c("system_id", "method", "count", "time", "success")]) #> tibble [48 × 5] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) #>  $ system_id: Factor w/ 24 levels "01_hcn","02_hcch",..: 1 1 2 2 3 3 14 14 4 4 ... #>  $ method   : Factor w/ 2 levels "CINEB","MMF": 1 2 1 2 1 2 1 2 1 2 ... #>  $ count    : int [1:48] 202 177 546 300 882 374 298 135 330 228 ... #>  $ time     : num [1:48] 6.06 5.66 11.13 8.23 16.45 ... #>  $ success  : logi [1:48] TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE ... tibble [48 x ...] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)  $ system_id: Factor w/ 24 levels ...  $ method   : Factor w/ 2 levels "CINEB","MMF": 1 2 1 2 ...  $ count    : int  202 177 546 300 882 374 ...  $ time     : num  6.06 5.66 11.13 8.23 ...  $ success  : logi  TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE ...

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: bc_fit() selects response family automatically (negative binomial integer counts, Gamma positive continuous times, Bernoulli binary success). sets weakly informative priors runs four MCMC chains via cmdstanr. model formula : generalized linear mixed model fixed effect method random intercept per test system, accounting fact reactions inherently harder others. Setting model_shape = TRUE adds shape submodel (shape ~ method) dispersion parameter can differ CI-NEB OCI-NEB. important one method may produce variable call counts . file argument caches compiled fitted model disk, subsequent runs load instantly. Note: Model fitting takes 2-5 minutes depending hardware. code block uses eval = FALSE MCMC sampling slow automated vignette builds. fitting completes, bayescomp automatically runs convergence diagnostics prints summary: low shape values (well 50) confirm substantial overdispersion, validating choice negative binomial Poisson.
code: count ~ method + (1 | system_id) fit_count <- bc_fit(   bench_long,   response  = "count",   model_shape = TRUE,   file      = "models/baker_count" ) v All convergence diagnostics passed (Rhat <= 1.01, ESS >= 400). i NB shape (reference): 1.3 (median on response scale) i NB shape (MMF): 2.1 [1.0, 4.5]

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: bc_summarize_effects() extracts posterior distribution method effect converts interpretable quantities: multiplicative factor percentage change response scale. Expected output: key result: OCI-NEB uses 46.4% fewer force/energy evaluations CI-NEB, 95% credible interval [-54.6%, -36.7%]. entire interval negative, strong evidence OCI-NEB efficient. publication-ready table:
code: effects <- bc_summarize_effects(fit_count) print(effects) # A tibble: 3 x 5   effect_type                      median  lower  upper formatted   <chr>                             <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr> 1 Expected Response (Baseline)     551.   331.   917.   551.23 [330.65, 917.42] 2 Multiplicative Factor (MMF)        0.54   0.45   0.63 0.54 [0.45, 0.63] 3 Percentage Change (MMF)          -46.4  -54.6  -36.7  -46.41 [-54.62, -36.73] bc_effect_table(fit_count) # A tibble: 3 x 3   Effect                           Median  `95% CrI`   <chr>                            <chr>   <chr> 1 Expected Response (Baseline)     551.23  [330.65, 917.42] 2 Multiplicative Factor (MMF)      0.54    [0.45, 0.63] 3 Percentage Change (MMF)          -46.4%  [-54.6%, -36.7%]

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: bc_plot_conditional() overlays model’s predicted response raw data, letting see well model captures pattern across test systems. resulting plot shows posterior mean 95% credible band method, raw per-system counts overlaid jittered points. log scale appropriate negative binomial model uses log link.
code: bc_plot_conditional(   fit_count,   effects      = "method",   data         = bench_long,   response_col = "count",   log_y        = TRUE )

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: Baker data includes RMSD_Init_Final (geometric dissimilarity reactant product), known affect difficulty NEB calculation. can include method-varying spline: adds s(RMSD_Init_Final, = method, k = 3) formula, letting method smooth relationship covariate. conditional effects plot shows predicted counts function RMSD, coloured method.
code: fit_spline <- bc_fit(   bench_long,   response         = "count",   spline_by_method = "RMSD_Init_Final",   model_shape      = TRUE,   file             = "models/baker_count_spline" ) bc_plot_conditional(   fit_spline,   effects      = "RMSD_Init_Final:method",   data         = bench_long,   response_col = "count",   log_y        = TRUE )

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: reproducible analyses, bayescomp integrates targets package. Place following _targets.R file: Run targets::tar_make() execute pipeline. Subsequent runs skip steps whose inputs changed.
code: library(targets) library(bayescomp)  list(   bc_tar_pipeline(     data_path       = "inst/extdata/baker_bench.csv",     method_suffixes = c("CINEB", "MMF"),     method_levels   = c("CINEB", "MMF"),     response        = "count",     system_col      = "System",     count_col       = "Calls",     time_col        = "Time",     success_col     = "Term",     model_shape     = FALSE,     model_file      = "data/models/baker_count"   ) )

title: Getting Started with bayescomp
text: typical bayescomp workflow : bc_read_benchmark() – load CSV data bc_pivot_long() – reshape wide long format bc_fit() – fit Bayesian hierarchical model bc_summarize_effects() bc_effect_table() – extract treatment effects bc_plot_conditional() – visualize model predictions diagnostics, model selection, multi-method comparisons, see companion vignettes: vignette("neb-case-study") – full NEB analysis diagnostics vignette("dimer-case-study") – multi-method comparison (>2 methods) vignette("model-selection") – family selection prior sensitivity

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: vignette analyses dimer rotation benchmark, compares four optimization methods initial dimer rotation step transition-state searches. data set three response variables (force/energy call counts, wall-clock time, binary success), making good test case bc_fit_suite(). also demonstrates two features go beyond basic bc_fit() workflow: bc_fit_design() factorial designs interaction terms bc_pairwise_contrasts() -pairs comparisons two methods

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: dimer rotation benchmark ships bayescomp. Unlike Baker data, already long format: one row per system-method observation. columns : system_id – identifier combining molecule index spin state method – one cg_no, cg_yes, lbfgs_no, lbfgs_yes (conjugate-gradient L-BFGS optimizer, without rotation removal) pes_calls – number potential energy surface evaluations (count) tot_time – wall-clock time seconds (positive continuous) success – logical success flag derived termination_status mol_id – molecule index (grouping variable) spin – spin state (doublets singlets) reference level cg_no (conjugate gradient without rotation removal), methods compared.
code: library(bayescomp) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union path <- system.file("extdata", "idrot_bench.csv", package = "bayescomp")  bench <- bc_read_benchmark(   path,   format     = "long",   system_col = "system_id" )  head(bench) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   system_id  method pes_calls tot_time success termination_status #>   <fct>      <chr>      <int>    <dbl> <chr>   <chr>              #> 1 0_doublets cg_no         75     333. True    GOOD               #> 2 0_singlets cg_no        154     583. True    GOOD               #> 3 1_doublets cg_no        389    1983. True    GOOD               #> 4 1_singlets cg_no        929    3322. True    GOOD               #> 5 2_doublets cg_no        244    1405. True    GOOD               #> 6 2_singlets cg_no        269    1094. True    GOOD               #> # ℹ 4 more variables: rmsd_init_saddle <dbl>, barrier <dbl>, mol_id <int>, #> #   spin <chr> bench$method <- factor(   bench$method,   levels = c("cg_no", "cg_yes", "lbfgs_no", "lbfgs_yes") )  table(bench$method) #>  #>     cg_no    cg_yes  lbfgs_no lbfgs_yes  #>       500       500       500       500

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
code: bc_validate(   bench,   count_col  = "pes_calls",   method_col = "method",   system_col = "system_id" )

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: first pass, can use bc_fit() count response: gives percentage change estimates method relative cg_no. However, four methods form 2x2 factorial design (optimizer x rotation removal), bc_fit() treats four independent levels. test interaction optimizer choice rotation removal, need bc_fit_design().
code: fit_simple <- bc_fit(   bench,   response    = "pes_calls",   method_col  = "method",   system_col  = "system_id",   model_shape = TRUE,   file        = "models/idrot_simple" )  bc_summarize_effects(fit_simple)

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: First, create factorial columns method labels: Now specify interaction formula using brms::bf(): interaction term optimizer:rotation_removal tells us whether benefit rotation removal depends optimizer use (vice versa).
code: bench <- bench %>%   mutate(     optimizer = factor(       ifelse(grepl("^cg", method), "CG", "LBFGS"),       levels = c("CG", "LBFGS")     ),     rotation_removal = factor(       ifelse(grepl("_yes$", method), "yes", "no"),       levels = c("no", "yes")     )   ) library(brms)  design_formula <- bf(   pes_calls ~ optimizer * rotation_removal + (1 | system_id) )  fit_design <- bc_fit_design(   bench,   formula = design_formula,   family  = negbinomial(link = "log"),   file    = "models/idrot_design" ) bc_summarize_effects(fit_design)

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: dimer data hierarchical grouping structure: molecule (mol_id) tested two spin states (spin), creating system_id = mol_id:spin compound key. can model explicitly: partitions variance molecule-level spin-state-level components. Use bc_loo_compare() check whether nested structure improves prediction (see model-selection vignette).
code: nested_formula <- bf(   pes_calls ~ optimizer * rotation_removal + (1 | mol_id) + (1 | mol_id:spin) )  fit_nested <- bc_fit_design(   bench,   formula = nested_formula,   family  = negbinomial(link = "log"),   file    = "models/idrot_nested" )

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: data includes count, time, success columns, bc_fit_suite() fits separate model , using appropriate family automatically: Negative binomial pes_calls (counts) Gamma tot_time (positive continuous) Bernoulli success (binary) Expected output: element brmsfit object. can extract effects :
code: # Ensure the success column is properly coded bench$success_bin <- as.integer(bench$success == "True" | bench$success == TRUE)  suite <- bc_fit_suite(   bench,   count_col   = "pes_calls",   time_col    = "tot_time",   success_col = "success_bin",   method_col  = "method",   system_col  = "system_id",   file_prefix = "models/idrot_suite" )  names(suite) [1] "count" "time" "success" lapply(suite, bc_summarize_effects)

title: Multi-Method Comparison with Dimer Rotation
text: four methods, six pairwise comparisons. bc_pairwise_contrasts() uses marginaleffects package compute single fitted model. Expected output (values illustrative): Contrasts whose credible intervals exclude zero provide strong evidence difference. Contrasts span zero inconclusive chosen credibility level.
code: contrasts <- bc_pairwise_contrasts(suite$count, method_col = "method") print(contrasts) # A tibble: 6 x 5   contrast             estimate  lower  upper formatted   <chr>                   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr> 1 cg_yes - cg_no         -85.2 -142.3  -28.1 -85.20 [-142.30, -28.10] 2 lbfgs_no - cg_no       -62.4 -118.7   -6.1 -62.40 [-118.70, -6.10] 3 lbfgs_yes - cg_no     -120.6 -176.9  -64.3 -120.60 [-176.90, -64.30] 4 lbfgs_no - cg_yes       22.8  -33.5   79.1 22.80 [-33.50, 79.10] 5 lbfgs_yes - cg_yes     -35.4  -91.7   20.9 -35.40 [-91.70, 20.90] 6 lbfgs_yes - lbfgs_no   -58.2 -114.5   -1.9 -58.20 [-114.50, -1.90]

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: Choosing right response family checking results depend prior assumptions two steps belong every Bayesian analysis. vignette covers: bc_suggest_family() picks family recommendations matter Comparing candidate models bc_loo_compare() Prior sensitivity analysis refitting different prior scales prefer Student-t Gaussian real-valued responses

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: use simulated data ground truth known. makes easier see modelling choices recover true effect. true effect log(0.6), corresponding 40% reduction counts method_2 relative method_1. shape parameter 5 implies substantial overdispersion (variance much larger mean).
code: library(bayescomp) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union set.seed(42)  sim_count <- bc_simulate_benchmark(   n_systems   = 30,   n_methods   = 2,   true_effect = log(0.6),   family      = "negbinomial",   shape       = 5,   intercept   = log(500),   system_sd   = 0.4 )  head(sim_count) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   system_id method   count #>   <fct>     <fct>    <dbl> #> 1 sys_01    method_1   976 #> 2 sys_02    method_1   541 #> 3 sys_03    method_1   346 #> 4 sys_04    method_1   740 #> 5 sys_05    method_1   572 #> 6 sys_06    method_1   435

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: bc_suggest_family() inspects response vector applies following rules: Expected output: variance nearly 100 times mean, ruling Poisson model (assumes variance equals mean). negative binomial adds shape parameter capture extra variance.
code: bc_suggest_family(sim_count$count) #> ℹ Count data detected. Using negative binomial (log link). #> ℹ Gaussian models produce misleading results for count data. #> ℹ Variance = 108909.5, mean = 475.3. #>  #> Family: negbinomial  #> Link function: log i Count data detected. Using negative binomial (log link). i Gaussian models produce misleading results for count data. i Variance = 37894.2, mean = 402.3.

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: common mistake modelling counts positive-data Gaussian family. bc_suggest_family() warns : problems Gaussian count data: identity link can predict negative counts Gaussian assumes symmetric, constant-variance errors, wrong counts (variance scales mean) Credible intervals become artificially narrow Gaussian likelihood confident wrong distributional shape
code: bc_suggest_family(sim_count$count, type = "gaussian") #> Warning: ! Data looks like non-negative integer counts. #> ℹ Gaussian models produce misleading results for count data. #> ℹ Consider `type = "count"` (negative binomial) instead. #>  #> Family: gaussian  #> Link function: identity ! Data looks like non-negative integer counts. i Gaussian models produce misleading results for count data. i Consider `type = "count"` (negative binomial) instead.

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: make case empirically, fit data negative binomial Gaussian families, compare using LOO-CV: Expected output: negative binomial model substantially higher expected log predictive density (elpd). difference -48.3 (SE 12.1) decisive: negative binomial fits data far better.
code: fit_nb <- bc_fit(   sim_count,   response = "count",   family   = brms::negbinomial(link = "log"),   file     = "models/sim_nb" )  fit_gaussian <- bc_fit(   sim_count,   response = "count",   family   = brms::brmsfamily("gaussian", link = "identity"),   file     = "models/sim_gaussian" )  bc_loo_compare(fit_nb, fit_gaussian, model_names = c("NegBin", "Gaussian")) i Computing LOO-CV for 2 models... v Preferred model: NegBin  # A tibble: 2 x 3   model    elpd_diff se_diff   <chr>        <dbl>   <dbl> 1 NegBin         0      0 2 Gaussian     -48.3    12.1

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: low-overdispersion data (shape 100), Poisson may suffice. bayescomp provide Poisson directly, can pass : shape = 5 (high overdispersion), Poisson model perform poorly. data shape 100, two models indistinguishable. NB shape parameter reported bc_fit() fitting tells regime :
code: fit_pois <- bc_fit(   sim_count,   response = "count",   family   = brms::poisson(link = "log"),   file     = "models/sim_poisson" )  bc_loo_compare(fit_nb, fit_pois, model_names = c("NegBin", "Poisson")) i NB shape: 5.2 [3.8, 7.1] -- high overdispersion

title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: bayescomp uses weakly informative priors default. key priors : check sensitivity, refit model tighter wider priors fixed effects: Compare effect estimates across prior specifications: Expected output (values illustrative): three estimates similar (overlapping credible intervals), results robust prior choice. Large shifts indicate data sparse overcome prior, either collect data report sensitivity explicitly. can also compare via LOO-CV: well-identified models 20+ systems, three similar elpd values.
code: library(brms)  # Tighter priors (more regularization) priors_tight <- c(   prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b"),   prior(exponential(1), class = "sd"),   prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "Intercept") )  # Wider priors (less regularization) priors_wide <- c(   prior(normal(0, 2), class = "b"),   prior(exponential(1), class = "sd"),   prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "Intercept") )  fit_default <- bc_fit(sim_count, response = "count", file = "models/sim_default")  fit_tight <- bc_fit(   sim_count,   response = "count",   prior    = priors_tight,   file     = "models/sim_tight" )  fit_wide <- bc_fit(   sim_count,   response = "count",   prior    = priors_wide,   file     = "models/sim_wide" ) bind_rows(   bc_summarize_effects(fit_default) %>% mutate(prior = "default (sd=1)"),   bc_summarize_effects(fit_tight)   %>% mutate(prior = "tight (sd=0.5)"),   bc_summarize_effects(fit_wide)    %>% mutate(prior = "wide (sd=2)") ) %>%   filter(grepl("Percentage", effect_type)) %>%   select(prior, median, lower, upper) # A tibble: 3 x 4   prior           median  lower  upper   <chr>            <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> 1 default (sd=1)   -39.8  -50.2  -27.4 2 tight (sd=0.5)   -38.1  -47.9  -26.5 3 wide (sd=2)      -40.2  -51.1  -27.8 bc_loo_compare(   fit_default, fit_tight, fit_wide,   model_names = c("default", "tight", "wide") )

what: When to use Student-t vs Gaussian
title: Model Selection and Prior Sensitivity
text: benchmark responses real-valued (e.g., energy differences, barrier errors). can negative, log link families (NB, Gamma) apply. Student-t heavier tails Gaussian, making robust occasional large error common computational chemistry benchmarks (e.g., method failing one system producing wildly wrong energy). degrees--freedom parameter (nu) estimated data; values near 3-5 indicate heavy tails, values 30 approach Gaussian behaviour. data truly heavy tails, Student-t model higher elpd. data well-behaved, two models perform similarly, lose nothing using Student-t.
code: sim_real <- bc_simulate_benchmark(   n_systems   = 30,   n_methods   = 2,   true_effect = -0.5,   family      = "student",   intercept   = 0,   system_sd   = 0.3,   sigma       = 1 )  head(sim_real) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   system_id method    value #>   <fct>     <fct>     <dbl> #> 1 sys_01    method_1  0.819 #> 2 sys_02    method_1  1.31  #> 3 sys_03    method_1 -1.16  #> 4 sys_04    method_1 -0.700 #> 5 sys_05    method_1  0.517 #> 6 sys_06    method_1 -1.86 bc_suggest_family(sim_real$value) #> ℹ Real-valued data with negatives detected. Using Student-t (identity link). #> ℹ Student-t is robust to outliers compared to Gaussian. #>  #> Family: student  #> Link function: identity i Real-valued data with negatives detected. Using Student-t (identity link). i Student-t is robust to outliers compared to Gaussian. fit_student <- bc_fit(   sim_real,   response = "value",   family   = brms::student(link = "identity"),   file     = "models/sim_student" )  fit_gauss <- bc_fit(   sim_real,   response = "value",   family   = brms::brmsfamily("gaussian", link = "identity"),   file     = "models/sim_gauss" )  bc_loo_compare(fit_student, fit_gauss, model_names = c("Student-t", "Gaussian"))

title: NEB Method Comparison

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Comparison of Computational Method Benchmarks
Summary
text: Comparing computational methods shared test problems central method development computational chemistry, materials science, related fields. Standard practice reduces per-system results mean ratios median speedups, discarding hierarchical structure data providing uncertainty quantification. bayescomp R package fits Bayesian hierarchical models benchmark comparisons, treating test system random effect selecting appropriate response family (negative binomial counts, Gamma timing, Bernoulli convergence success, Student-t signed errors) opinionated guardrails common misspecifications applying Gaussian models count data.

title: Summary
text: Computational benchmarks produce count data (force evaluations, SCF iterations), wall-clock times, binary convergence outcomes across tens hundreds test systems. survey Journal Chemical Theory Computation (JCTC) Journal Chemical Physics (JCP) confirms formal statistical testing essentially absent literature: results reported tables raw numbers mean median summary rows. practice three problems. First, ignores paired structure: test system baseline difficulty, method differences confounded test-set composition. Second, count data overdispersed (variance exceeds mean, often order magnitude), violating Gaussian assumptions. Third, small benchmark sets (N = 20–50 systems) make frequentist p-values unreliable point estimates noisy. bayescomp addresses three wrapping brms [@Buerkner2017brms] domain-appropriate defaults. two-function API — bc_fit() simple -vs-B comparisons bc_fit_design() factorial designs — auto-detects response family, sets weakly informative priors, handles right-censored observations (runs hitting iteration limits), reports convergence diagnostics automatically. package integrates targets [@Landau2021targets] reproducible pipelines provides 15 publication-quality plot types including dumbbell comparisons, performance profiles, posterior predictive checks following Bayesian workflow recommendations @Gelman2020workflow @Gabry2019visualization.

title: Summary
text: Opinionated family selection: auto-detects negative binomial/Gamma/Bernoulli/Student-t data type, warns Gaussian misuse count data Right-censoring: runs hitting iteration limits treated censored observations via brms | cens(), preventing downward bias Two entry points: bc_fit() common case (method factor), bc_fit_design() full brms::bf() formula passthrough Pairwise contrasts: bc_pairwise_contrasts() wraps marginaleffects >2 method comparisons Simulation: bc_simulate_benchmark() power analysis parameter recovery testing 40 exported functions covering data ingestion, validation, model fitting, diagnostics, effect extraction, visualization

title: Summary
text: package ships data Baker transition-state benchmark (24 molecular systems, CI-NEB vs OCI-NEB). complete analysis: yields 46% reduction gradient evaluations (95% CrI: 37–55%) OCI-NEB relative CI-NEB, full posterior uncertainty quantification [@Goswami2025dimer].
code: library(bayescomp) data <- bc_read_benchmark(   system.file("extdata", "baker_bench.csv", package = "bayescomp"),   format = "wide", method_suffixes = c("CINEB", "MMF")) long <- bc_pivot_long(data, method_pattern = "_(CINEB|MMF)$",                        method_levels = c("CINEB", "MMF")) model <- bc_fit(long, response = "count",                  spline_by_method = "RMSD_Init_Final",                  model_shape = TRUE) bc_report(model)

title: Simulate benchmark comparison data — bc_simulate_benchmark
code: bc_simulate_benchmark(   n_systems = 20,   n_methods = 2,   true_effect = log(0.5),   family = c("negbinomial", "gamma", "bernoulli", "student"),   shape = 50,   intercept = NULL,   system_sd = 0.3,   sigma = 1,   cens_threshold = NULL,   seed = 42 )

title: Simulate benchmark comparison data — bc_simulate_benchmark
text: n_systems Number test systems (default 20). n_methods Number methods (default 2). Methods named "method_1", "method_2", etc. first method reference. true_effect True log-scale effect non-reference methods relative reference. negbinomial/Gamma, log scale (e.g., log(0.5) means 50% reduction). Bernoulli, logit scale. Scalar (non-reference methods) vector length n_methods - 1. family One "negbinomial", "gamma", "bernoulli", "student". shape Shape parameter negbinomial Gamma (default 50). intercept Intercept link scale (default log(500) negbinomial/Gamma, 0 Bernoulli/Student-t). system_sd Standard deviation random intercept per system (default 0.3 link scale). sigma Residual SD Student-t family (default 1). cens_threshold Right-censoring threshold. NULL, observations value censored censored column (0/1) added. seed Random seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate benchmark comparison data — bc_simulate_benchmark
text: tibble columns: system_id (factor), method (factor), response column named count (negbinomial), time (Gamma), success (Bernoulli), value (Student-t). cens_threshold set, also includes censored (integer 0/1).

what: Suggest appropriate brms family based on response variable — bc_suggest_family
title: Suggest appropriate brms family based on response variable — bc_suggest_family
text: Examines response vector suggests appropriate brms family. Provides opinionated guidance: count data gets negative binomial (Gaussian), positive continuous gets Gamma, binary gets Bernoulli, real-valued (negatives) gets Student-t. Warns Gaussian count positive-data, since Gaussian assumptions produce misleading results cases (see Goswami 2025, arXiv:2505.13621).

title: Suggest appropriate brms family based on response variable — bc_suggest_family
code: bc_suggest_family(   y,   type = c("auto", "count", "time", "success", "real", "gaussian") )

title: Suggest appropriate brms family based on response variable — bc_suggest_family
text: y Response vector. type One "auto", "count", "time", "success", "real", "gaussian". "auto", type inferred y. "real" selects Student-t signed real-valued data (energy errors, barrier differences). "gaussian" forces Gaussian warns data looks like counts positive-.

title: Suggest appropriate brms family based on response variable — bc_suggest_family
text: brms family object.
